sections.heading: RT
sections.content:
RT is indicated for nearly all cases of high-risk neuroblastoma and in exceedingly rare circumstances for patients with non–high-risk disease. For example, symptomatic patients (stage MS) with severe ongoing organ dysfunction with inadequate response to initial chemotherapy may benefit from RT; multidisciplinary discussion is recommended [NEUROB-H 1 of 3]. The primary site is always irradiated. Metastatic sites are only irradiated if there is concern for active disease after completion of induction chemotherapy, demonstrated by MIBG/FDG uptake and/or persistent soft-tissue mass >1 cm³ [NEUROB-H 1 of 3].

Principles:
- RT will be delivered after recovery from high-dose chemotherapy with stem cell rescue unless there is an indication for emergent RT [NEUROB-H 1 of 3].
- Intensity-modulated RT (IMRT) or proton therapy can be used to reduce the risk and/or magnitude of side effects [NEUROB-H 1 of 3].
- If radiation to metastatic sites is indicated, they should be irradiated concurrently with the primary site [NEUROB-H 1 of 3].
- The Panel recognizes that not all metastatic sites may be feasibly targeted by external beam radiotherapy [NEUROB-H 1 of 3].
- Boosting residual disease after surgery to 36 Gy did not improve cumulative incidence of local progression [NEUROB-H 2 of 3, footnote a].
- The Panel does not recommend extending radiotherapy fields to include uninvolved draining nodal stations (A3973 data) or augmenting dose to gross residual tumor (ANBL0532 data) [NEUROB-D 5 of 13].

Dose frameworks:
- Primary site and involved regional lymph nodes: 21.6 Gy total, 1.8 Gy/fraction, 12 fractions, delivered after recovery from high-dose chemotherapy with stem cell rescue. Standard dose for primary site irradiation in high-risk neuroblastoma [NEUROB-H 2 of 3].
- Metastatic sites concerning for active disease: 21.6 Gy total, 1.8 Gy/fraction, 12 fractions, delivered concurrently with primary site RT after transplant recovery. Sites with MIBG/FDG uptake or persistent soft-tissue mass >1 cm³ at end of induction [NEUROB-H 2 of 3].
- Emergent RT: 4.5 Gy total, 1.5 Gy/fraction, 3 fractions, emergent setting. Indications: Hepatomegaly leading to respiratory distress; orbit or optic pathway disease leading to vision loss [NEUROB-H 2 of 3].

Approaches:
- Primary site consolidative RT: 21.6 Gy in 1.8 Gy/fraction (12 fractions). No concurrent chemotherapy. Delivered after recovery from high-dose chemotherapy with stem cell rescue. Key trials: ANBL0532 (boosting to 36 Gy did not improve local control), A3973 (extending fields not beneficial). Toxicities: organ-specific constraints (kidney V18 Gy <25% contralateral, mean ≤14.4 Gy; liver mean <18 Gy; vertebrae min 18 Gy; bilateral lungs V20 Gy <30%) [NEUROB-H 2 of 3].
- Metastatic site RT: 21.6 Gy in 1.8 Gy/fraction (12 fractions). No concurrent chemotherapy. Indication: residual metastatic disease at end of induction. Key trials: Casey et al. 2018, Polishchuk et al. 2014. Toxicities: site-dependent.
- Emergent RT: 4.5 Gy in 1.5 Gy/fraction (3 fractions). No concurrent chemotherapy. Indication: hepatomegaly with respiratory distress, orbit/optic pathway disease leading to vision loss [NEUROB-H 2 of 3].

timeline.title: Primary site RT
timeline.body: 21.6 Gy in 12 fractions of 1.8 Gy each, after recovery from high-dose chemotherapy with stem cell rescue.

timeline.title: Metastatic site RT
timeline.body: 21.6 Gy in 12 fractions of 1.8 Gy each, concurrently with primary site RT after transplant recovery.

timeline.title: Emergent RT
timeline.body: 4.5 Gy in 3 fractions of 1.5 Gy each, for hepatomegaly or optic pathway compromise.

timeline.title: Boost (not recommended)
timeline.body: 36 Gy boost to residual disease after surgery did not improve local progression [ANBL0532].
